name: Libertador General Bernardo O'Higgins
history: Libertador General Bernardo O'Higgins Region, named after Chile's independence hero, was originally inhabited by the Mapuche and other indigenous peoples before Spanish conquest in the 16th century. The region became a center of agricultural development during the colonial period, with large estates and vineyards establishing its economic foundation. Following Chilean independence in the early 19th century, the region continued to grow as an important producer of wine, fruit, and grain, shaping modern central Chilean commerce. The construction of railroads and modern infrastructure in the 20th century further integrated the region into the national economy.
culturalNotes: The region is deeply rooted in Chilean huaso culture, with strong traditions of horsemanship, rodeo, and rural agricultural life that remain central to local identity. Wine production is a defining characteristic, with numerous vineyards and wineries attracting visitors and contributing significantly to both local pride and the economy. Festivals celebrating harvest seasons, patron saints, and national independence are important communal events that reflect the region's blend of indigenous heritage and Spanish colonial traditions.
bestTimeToVisit: Visit February to April to enjoy wine season and harvest festivals in this major wine region.
languageNotes: Spanish is the primary language, and locals speak with the characteristic Chilean accent and cadence, featuring distinctive pronunciation and colloquialisms common to central Chile. Useful phrases include "Gracias por su hospitalidad" (thank you for your hospitality) and asking about local wines with "¿Qué vinos recomiendan de esta zona?" (What wines do you recommend from this area?).

places.name: Rancagua
places.description: The vibrant capital of the O'Higgins Region, Rancagua is a modern city with rich history dating back to the colonial era and the Chilean independence wars. It serves as the economic and cultural hub of the region with excellent museums, plazas, and access to nearby wine valleys and hot springs.

activities.detail: The main square of Rancagua surrounded by colonial architecture, shops, and cafes, serving as the heart of the city.
A regional museum housed in a restored colonial mansion displaying pre-Columbian artifacts, colonial art, and independence war memorabilia.
A historic 18th-century Baroque church featuring ornate interiors and bell towers, representing religious heritage of the region.
Home stadium of Chilean football club O'Higgins, offering stadium tours and local sporting events.
A well-maintained English-style park offering green space, walking paths, and recreational areas for relaxation in the city center.
A nearby wine region just outside the city featuring wineries and vineyards producing Carmenère and Cabernet Sauvignon varietals.
Natural hot springs resort located 45 minutes from Rancagua in the Andes foothills, ideal for relaxation and wellness.

food.detail: A traditional Chilean corn pie with ground meat filling, topped with fresh corn puree and baked until golden, served as a comfort food staple.
Fried pastry pockets filled with seasoned ground beef, onions, olives, and hard-boiled eggs, eaten as a common street snack or light meal.
Fresh conger eel baked or grilled with butter, garlic, and white wine, reflecting the region's access to Pacific seafood.
A hearty chicken and vegetable stew served in an earthenware pot, featuring potatoes, corn, and squash as traditional regional comfort food.
The nearby renowned wine region produces world-class Carmenère and Cabernet Sauvignon that define the area's culinary identity.

culture.detail: Rancagua is deeply connected to Chilean independence history, so approach colonial buildings and war memorials with reverence and take time to learn their significance.
Many local businesses close between 1 and 4 PM for lunch and rest, so plan afternoon activities accordingly and enjoy a leisurely midday meal like locals.
When visiting nearby wineries, dress neatly and arrive for scheduled tours; tastings are taken seriously and tipping is appreciated but not obligatory.
While some tourism workers speak English, speaking basic Spanish significantly enhances interactions and shows respect for local culture in this primarily Spanish-speaking city.

places.name: Pichilemu
places.description: A picturesque Pacific coastal town famous for its excellent surfing beaches and dramatic cliff-side scenery. Pichilemu offers a laid-back atmosphere with fresh seafood, artisan shops, and stunning sunsets over dramatic rock formations.

activities.detail: Ride the consistent Pacific swells at Pichilemu's most famous break, a world-class right-hand point break popular with surfers of all levels.
Walk the main beach with its distinctive rock formations and dramatic cliffs, perfect for swimming, sunbathing, and watching the sunset.
Browse local crafts, handmade jewelry, and artwork at the waterfront market showcasing work from regional artisans.
Trek up to the historic lighthouse perched on dramatic cliffs for panoramic ocean views and photo opportunities of the rugged coastline.
Explore tidal pools filled with starfish, sea urchins, and anemones along this scenic cove adjacent to the main beach.
Paddle along the coastline to discover hidden sea caves and explore the dramatic rocky outcrops from water level.

food.detail: Locally caught fish cured in citrus juice with onions and cilantro, a signature dish at waterfront restaurants showcasing the day's catch.
Baked pastries filled with shrimp, clams, and fish in creamy sauce, a popular handheld snack from local bakeries.
A traditional Chilean crab and seafood pie with a savory filling baked in pastry, commonly served at family restaurants.
Razor clams topped with cheese and baked, a regional specialty highlighting the area's abundant shellfish.
Fried conger eel served whole or filleted, a coastal Chilean staple that's crispy outside and tender inside.

culture.detail: Pichilemu is a serious surfing destination; be mindful of locals' wave priorities and follow beach break etiquette if you paddle out.
January and February bring the annual Festival de Música and surfing competitions; plan accordingly if you want to experience local events or prefer quieter months.
While some tourism workers speak English, Pichilemu remains a relatively quiet town where Spanish is essential for deeper interactions with locals and artisans.
Locals gather at Playa Principal and the waterfront at dusk; joining this unhurried social custom is a genuine way to experience community life.

places.name: Santa Cruz
places.description: A charming wine country town nestled in the Colchagua Valley, Chile's premier wine region known for Carmenere production. Santa Cruz combines colonial architecture, excellent wineries, and the renowned Colchagua Museum showcasing regional history and pre-Columbian artifacts.

activities.detail: Explore one of Chile's most important regional museums housing pre-Columbian artifacts, colonial relics, and exhibits on Colchagua Valley history.
Visit nearby wineries specializing in Carmenere and other varietals, with tastings and vineyard tours available at estates like Santa Cruz Wine Route members.
Stroll through Santa Cruz's central square surrounded by colonial architecture, local shops, and a welcoming atmosphere typical of Chilean town centers.
Experience traditional Chilean countryside life by visiting working haciendas in the valley where you can ride horses and observe rural agricultural practices.
Tour this acclaimed winery known for architectural design and premium Carmenere production, located in the heart of the Colchagua Valley.
Browse traditional Chilean crafts, ceramics, and local products in Santa Cruz's town shops and weekend markets.

food.detail: Sample the signature red wine of the region produced locally, characterized by dark fruit flavors and spice notes unique to Colchagua terroir.
Enjoy this traditional Chilean corn pie layered with ground meat, onions, and topped with fresh corn puree, commonly served in local restaurants.
Taste handmade Chilean pastries filled with melted cheese and mushrooms, a popular local snack sold in bakeries throughout Santa Cruz.
Savor this hearty Chilean chicken stew with potatoes, corn, and pumpkin, reflecting the agricultural bounty of the valley.
Visit Santa Cruz's markets to purchase and taste fresh fruits, vegetables, and herbs grown in the fertile Colchagua Valley.

culture.detail: Many shops and services close between 1 and 3 p.m. for afternoon rest, so plan major activities or shopping accordingly.
While tourist areas have English speakers, learning basic Spanish phrases enhances interactions with locals and improves your experience in this charming Chilean town.

culture.text: Wine is central to local identity
culture.detail: The region takes pride in its wine heritage and Carmenere production, so showing genuine interest in local viticulture is appreciated by residents and winery staff.
Santa Cruz embodies Chilean warmth and relaxed valley life, so embrace the unhurried rhythm and accept invitations to local gatherings when offered.

places.name: Colchagua Valley
places.description: One of South America's most important wine regions, the Colchagua Valley stretches across rolling hills with world-class wineries producing exceptional Carmenere, Cabernet Sauvignon, and other varietals. Visitors can tour vineyards, taste award-winning wines, and enjoy farm-to-table dining experiences.

activities.detail: Visit one of Colchagua's flagship wineries known for premium Carmenere and Cabernet Sauvignon with guided tastings in colonial-style cellars.
Drive or cycle through the scenic valley floor visiting multiple family-owned vineyards producing award-winning wines with mountain backdrops.
Explore this museum in Santa Cruz displaying pre-Columbian artifacts, colonial art, and exhibits on the region's agricultural and viticultural history.
Tour this architecturally striking modernist winery carved into the hillside with barrel tastings and views across the Colchagua Valley.
Ride through working vineyard estates on traditional Chilean horses, a popular way to experience the landscape like local farmers.
Browse the bustling central market in Santa Cruz for local produce, cheese, and handicrafts while meeting regional vendors.

food.detail: A traditional Chilean casserole layering ground meat, onions, and olives topped with creamed corn, often paired with local wines.
Baked pastries filled with local cheese and wild mushrooms foraged from the Andes foothills surrounding the valley.
Multi-course meals at vineyard restaurants featuring seasonal valley produce matched with the estate's signature wines.
Grilled meats and vegetables cooked over open fire, a traditional Chilean barbecue experience common at winery gathering spaces.
A hearty chicken stew with potatoes, corn, and pumpkin, a comfort dish often served at rural ranch restaurants in the region.

culture.detail: Plan visits during late summer when harvest celebrations fill the valley; many wineries offer vendimia (harvest) tours and special events.
While larger wineries have English-speaking guides, smaller family operations primarily conduct tours in Spanish, so basic phrases enhance the experience.
Adopt the local rhythm of a long midday meal (1-3 PM) when many businesses close; plan winery visits for morning or late afternoon instead.
Chileans value personal connection; arriving with genuine interest in winemakers' stories and families opens doors to more intimate tastings and experiences.

places.name: Baños de Cauquenes
places.description: Historic natural hot springs nestled in the Andes foothills, surrounded by scenic mountain landscapes and native forests. These geothermal baths offer relaxation and wellness in an authentic Chilean countryside setting.
imageQueries: Baños de Cauquenes hot springs Chile

activities.detail: Immerse yourself in the geothermal pools fed by natural mineral springs heated to therapeutic temperatures ranging from 35°C to 40°C.
Explore well-maintained paths through native Andean forests featuring southern beech and cypress trees with mountain vistas throughout.
Trek to a nearby waterfall within the resort grounds where mountain spring water cascades through the forested landscape.
Ride through the Andes foothills on marked trails that offer varying difficulty levels and stunning valley panoramas.
Experience mud wraps and massages using local geothermal minerals and native plant extracts at the resort spa facilities.
Observe endemic Chilean bird species including Andean condors and woodpeckers in their natural mountain habitat.
Capture the dramatic Andes foothills, native forests, and geothermal features during guided photography sessions at optimal lighting times.

food.detail: Savory hand pies filled with seasoned ground beef, onions, raisins, and hard-boiled egg, a classic Chilean countryside staple.
Locally-caught Andean trout prepared simply grilled or pan-seared, highlighting the pristine mountain water sources.
A hearty vegetable stew made with seasonal produce from the region, traditionally served in a clay pot.
A Chilean-style hot dog loaded with avocado, tomato, onion, and mayonnaise, widely available at local vendors.
A comfort food casserole featuring ground beef and chicken topped with a sweet corn crust, popular in rural O'Higgins region.
A refreshing traditional beverage combining cooked wheat grains with dried peach, perfect after soaking in the hot springs.

culture.detail: The springs are natural and sacred to locals; avoid using soap or chemicals in the pools and follow all posted conservation guidelines.

culture.text: Arrive Early for Hot Spring Access
culture.detail: Peak bathing times are mornings and early evenings; arriving at dawn provides peaceful solitude and the best natural mineral water temperature.
While the resort staff speaks English, locals in the countryside primarily speak Spanish; learning simple greetings enhances your interaction with the community.

culture.text: Dress in Layers for Mountain Weather
culture.detail: The Andes foothills experience rapid temperature changes; bring layers even in summer as mornings and evenings are cool despite daytime warmth.

places.name: Laguna del Toro
places.description: A pristine Andean lake surrounded by native forests and mountain vistas, offering peaceful hiking and nature exploration opportunities. The area provides authentic outdoor experiences away from coastal tourist crowds with excellent bird watching possibilities.

activities.detail: A moderate 8-10 km trail circumnavigating the pristine alpine lake with views of the surrounding Andes peaks and native southern beech forests.
Spot endemic species including the Magellanic woodpecker, Andean condor, and various raptors during early morning expeditions around the lake's quieter northern shore.

activities.text: Forest Bathing in Native Ñirre Groves
activities.detail: Immerse yourself in the therapeutic atmosphere of the native southern beech forests that frame the lake, accessible via gentle walking paths.

activities.text: Fishing at the Lake's Eastern Inlet
activities.detail: Try fly-fishing for introduced trout species in the clear, cold waters where the inlet streams feed the main lake.
Reach the elevated viewpoint on the western slope for panoramic shots of the lake framed by jagged Andean peaks at sunrise or sunset.
Settle at one of the designated clearing areas near the southern shore for a peaceful lunch with unobstructed mountain vistas.

food.detail: The region's cold mountain waters yield excellent wild trout, often prepared simply grilled with local herbs at nearby estancias or lodges.
A traditional Mapuche and southern Chilean dish of seafood, meat, and potatoes cooked in an earth oven, sometimes adapted with local freshwater fish in this inland region.
Handmade pastry pockets filled with local cheese and wild mushrooms foraged from the surrounding forests, a common roadside snack in the area.
A loaded Chilean hot dog topped with avocado, tomato, mayonnaise, and sauerkraut, readily available at small vendors in nearby towns like Pucón.
Local produce prepared with merkén, a smoky Mapuche chili spice blend, reflecting indigenous culinary traditions of the O'Higgins region.

culture.detail: The lands surrounding Laguna del Toro hold cultural significance for the Mapuche people; visitors should be respectful and educate themselves about indigenous history.
This pristine area maintains strict leave-no-trace ethics, so bring all supplies and remove all waste to preserve the wilderness experience for future visitors.
Winter snow (June-August) can limit road access and hiking opportunities, so plan visits for late spring through early autumn for optimal conditions.
Hiring a knowledgeable local guide enhances bird-watching success and provides insights into regional ecology and Mapuche land relationships that independent travelers might miss.

places.name: Marchihue
places.description: A quiet coastal village offering an authentic small-town Chilean experience with fishing traditions and fresh seafood markets. It provides a more relaxed alternative to busier beach towns while maintaining coastal charm and local culture.

activities.detail: Visit the early-morning fish auction where local fishermen sell their daily catch directly to buyers, offering a genuine glimpse into the village's fishing heritage.
Stroll along the sandy beach to observe traditional fishing boats and enjoy the quiet coastal landscape away from tourist crowds.
Explore small family-run workshops where craftspeople create fishing nets, wooden boats, and traditional coastal handicrafts using ancestral techniques.
Dine at modest, family-owned eateries where chefs prepare the day's catch in simple, traditional Chilean coastal style.
Take a short trip to observe local bird species and native wetland vegetation in the protected area surrounding the village.

food.detail: Fresh conger eel baked whole in a salt crust, a traditional Chilean coastal preparation that seals in the delicate flavor of fish caught that morning.
Raw white fish cured in lemon juice with onions, cilantro, and local peppers, showcasing the village's access to pristine seafood.
Hand-made pastry pockets filled with mixed shellfish, often prepared by local women using family recipes passed down through generations.
A creamy crab and potato soup traditionally thickened with bread and enriched with local cheese, served as a warming weekday staple.
Razor clams topped with cheese and breadcrumbs then baked, a simple preparation highlighting the sweet meat of clams harvested locally.

culture.detail: Plan visits around dawn and dusk when fishermen are most active; afternoons are quiet as locals rest after morning labor.
Many family-run eateries and shops operate on cash-only basis, so arrive with Chilean pesos in smaller denominations.
English is rarely spoken in this authentic village, and locals appreciate visitors who make effort to communicate in Spanish, even with simple phrases.
Respect fishermen and locals' privacy by asking before photographing people, boats, or family workshops, as many value discretion.

places.name: Las Cabras
places.description: A rural town in the heart of O'Higgins wine country, serving as a gateway to numerous small wineries and agricultural experiences. Las Cabras offers genuine rural hospitality and access to lesser-known vineyard tours off the main tourist circuit.

activities.detail: A family-owned winery offering intimate tastings and tours through vineyards producing Carmenère and Cabernet Sauvignon in a relaxed rural setting.
A mid-sized producer known for experimental winemaking and cave tastings, accessible directly from Las Cabras without crossing into tourist-heavy zones.

activities.text: Rural horseback riding through vineyard estates
activities.detail: Local guides offer multi-hour rides through working wine properties and surrounding countryside, combining authentic farm experience with landscape views.
Self-guided or guided tours of the surrounding valley's wine routes, stopping at smaller operations and agricultural sites between Las Cabras and neighboring towns.
Weekly produce and artisanal goods market where residents and small producers sell seasonal vegetables, cheeses, and prepared foods in town center.

activities.text: Agricultural farm stays and agritourism experiences
activities.detail: Several local families offer day visits or overnight stays with hands-on activities like harvesting, cheese-making, or cooking with farm-fresh ingredients.

food.detail: Golden pastry pockets filled with local cheese and raisins, commonly made fresh by home cooks and available at small local bakeries on market days.

food.text: Cazuela de ave with Colchagua wine reduction
food.detail: A traditional Chilean stew of chicken, corn, and vegetables prepared locally using regional wine as a braising liquid.

food.text: Fresh choclo (giant Andean corn) with local cheese
food.detail: Large kerneled corn boiled and served with creamy local cheeses from rural dairies, a seasonal summer staple in the region.

food.text: Pastel de choclo at farm restaurants
food.detail: A savory pie layered with meat, hard-boiled eggs, and olives, topped with sweet corn puree, prepared using ingredients from nearby farms.
Local vineyards and farm lodges offer evening grilled meat services paired with their own wines, showcasing both agricultural products in one experience.

food.text: Freshly pressed grape juice during harvest season
food.detail: September through April, visitors can purchase or taste newly pressed juice directly from small family wineries during the grape harvest.

culture.text: Spanish is essential; English is rare
culture.detail: Outside of larger wine operations, very few people speak English, so basic Spanish phrases or a translation app are necessary for meaningful interaction.
Many local businesses close 1–4 PM for lunch and rest, and activities often begin early morning; plan accordingly and embrace a slower rhythm.

culture.text: Respect private property and ask permission
culture.detail: Much of the landscape is working agricultural land; always ask farmers or locals before walking through vineyards or rural roads to avoid trespassing.

culture.text: Harvest season (February–April) brings peak activity and community spirit
culture.detail: If visiting during grape harvest, you'll witness intense labor and celebratory gatherings among locals; roads may be busier and accommodations fuller.

places.name: Bucalemu
places.description: A small but appealing beach town and surfer haven south of Pichilemu with a quieter vibe and excellent coastal scenery. Bucalemu attracts surf enthusiasts and travelers seeking uncrowded beaches with consistent waves.

activities.detail: Ride consistent Pacific swells on the main beach break, which offers reliable waves for intermediate and advanced surfers year-round.
Hike to the rocky headland south of town for dramatic cliff views, tide pools, and opportunities to spot sea birds and seals.
Stroll the quieter sandy beach north of Bucalemu village, known for its white sand and calm stretches ideal for swimming and photography.
Walk along rocky inlets where artisanal fishermen work daily, offering insight into traditional small-scale fishing practices of the region.
Find a vantage point in the village or along the coastal path to watch the Pacific sunset paint the sky and ocean in warm colors.
Paddle in calm mornings to explore sea caves, hidden coves, and marine life along Bucalemu's protected coastline.

food.detail: A locally caught white fish served grilled or in ceviches at waterfront restaurants, celebrated for its delicate, slightly sweet flavor.
Fried pastries filled with mixed seafood, local cheese, and seasonings, sold at small bakeries and market stalls throughout the village.
A hearty stew combining mussels, clams, squid, and fish in a tomato and seafood broth, served in an earthenware pot at family-run restaurants.
A delicacy prepared with fresh sea urchin roe, lemon juice, and cilantro, showcasing the unique flavors of Chile's Pacific harvest.

food.text: Pan de Agua with Local Cheese
food.detail: Crusty water bread paired with fresh artisanal cheese from nearby farms, a simple breakfast or lunch staple in the town.

culture.text: Respect Surf Conditions and Local Surfers
culture.detail: The beach is a tight-knit surfer community; learn local break etiquette and avoid crowded peaks during peak hours to maintain harmony.
Bucalemu thrives on family-run hostels, restaurants, and shops; direct spending here sustains the local economy more than chain services.

culture.text: Low-Key, Quiet Evenings are the Norm
culture.detail: The town has minimal nightlife compared to larger resorts; embrace the early-to-bed rhythm, local gatherings, and connection to nature that define Bucalemu's appeal.
English is limited in Bucalemu; even simple Spanish phrases earn warmth from locals and enhance your experience in this intimate coastal village.

places.name: Artisan Route
places.description: A collection of small artisan workshops and galleries scattered around Pichilemu where local craftspeople produce traditional textiles, pottery, and handicrafts. This route provides insight into authentic regional craftsmanship and supports local artisans directly.

activities.detail: Watch local potters at work creating traditional Pichilemu ceramic pieces using techniques passed down through generations.
Tour small studios where artisans hand-dye and weave textiles using natural dyes sourced from local plants and minerals.
Buy handcrafted pottery, woven blankets, and wooden items directly from makers' workshops at fair prices without middleman markup.
Experience the seasonal Plaza de Pichilemu artisan markets where craftspeople display and sell their latest works alongside regional food vendors.
Join hands-on workshops offered by local weavers to learn traditional loom work and natural fiber preparation methods.
Combine artisan visits with a walk along Pichilemu's coastline, connecting workshops while enjoying ocean views and local beaches.

food.detail: Baked pastries filled with local cheese and wild coastal herbs, commonly sold by vendors near artisan workshops.
Savory corn pie layered with beef, onions, and hard-boiled egg, a traditional O'Higgins region comfort food served in local cafés.
Fresh conger eel stew with potatoes and cilantro, reflecting Pichilemu's Pacific fishing heritage and available at coastal restaurants.
Rustic artisan bread baked daily by local bakeries using traditional methods, perfect paired with fresh seafood or cheese.
A refreshing drink combining cooked wheat grains with dried peaches in sweet syrup, traditional Chilean fare especially popular in warmer months.

culture.detail: Purchasing directly from workshop owners ensures money goes to the craftspeople themselves and allows meaningful conversation about their techniques.
Most artisan workshops keep informal hours and may close for lunch or family time, so visit in mornings and confirm opening times locally.
Knowing basic Spanish words like 'telar' (loom), 'cerámica' (pottery), and 'tinte natural' (natural dye) deepens conversations with artisans.
Rural artisan workshops often operate on cash-only basis, so carry Chilean pesos in small denominations for purchases and transactions.

restaurants.name: Casa Tótem
restaurants.description: Upscale dining showcasing regional ingredients with modern Chilean techniques in an elegant setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Pastel de choclo with local corn and beef

restaurants.name: El Huerto
restaurants.description: Farm-to-table restaurant emphasizing seasonal vegetables and organic ingredients from their own garden.

restaurants.name: Asador Cono Sur
restaurants.description: Authentic parrilla serving perfectly grilled beef and lamb with traditional Argentine sides and chimichurri.

restaurants.name: La Viña
restaurants.description: Lively tapas bar offering classic Spanish small plates and an extensive wine selection from local vineyards.

restaurants.name: Siete Hermanos
restaurants.description: Casual coastal-style restaurant featuring fresh daily catches and traditional fish preparations.
restaurants.mustOrder: Congrio a la mantequilla (conger eel in butter sauce)

restaurants.name: Quinta los Morales
restaurants.description: Rural quinta offering authentic Chilean country cooking with ingredients from their working farm.

restaurants.name: Fuego Patagónico
restaurants.description: Premium grilled meats featuring Patagonian lamb and centolla (king crab) in a warm, rustic setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Cordero patagónico with regional red wine reduction

restaurants.name: Pizzería Don Quixote
restaurants.description: Casual neighborhood pizzeria with wood-fired oven serving authentic Neapolitan-style pizzas.
restaurants.mustOrder: Pizza al tartufo with local mushrooms

restaurants.name: Viña Santa Cruz Restaurant
restaurants.address: Avenida Viña Santa Cruz s/n, Santa Cruz
restaurants.description: Wine estate restaurant paired with the historic Santa Cruz vineyard, offering cuisine designed to complement their wines.

restaurants.name: Mariscos al Paso
restaurants.description: Quick-service seafood stand offering fresh ceviche, empanadas, and grilled fish with vibrant flavors.
restaurants.mustOrder: Ceviche mixto with fresh lemon and cilantro

restaurants.name: Casa Blanca Wine Bar
restaurants.description: Sophisticated wine bar featuring Mediterranean cuisine and curated selections from Chilean producers.
restaurants.mustOrder: Seared scallops with white wine and garlic

restaurants.name: El Tempranillo
restaurants.description: Refined Spanish restaurant showcasing modern takes on traditional dishes with premium ingredients.
restaurants.mustOrder: Pulpo a la Gallega with paprika-infused olive oil

restaurants.name: Las Trancas
restaurants.description: Rural-style restaurant known for traditional Chilean barbecue and rustic countryside hospitality.
restaurants.mustOrder: Asado de tira with grilled corn and beans

restaurants.name: Sotto Terra
restaurants.description: Intimate underground restaurant offering handmade pasta and sophisticated Italian cuisine with Chilean wine pairings.
restaurants.mustOrder: Pappardelle al ragù with truffle shavings
